title: The PH Constitution and the Running Bulls of Technology: (AI, Genetic Engineering, Nanotech and Info)

audience_match: National Historical Commission of the Philippines and National Museum of the Philippines leadership
Constitutional scholars, lawyers, judges, and law students
Conference and Exhibit attendees on 'Philippine Constitutions and the Social Order'
Civic readers concerned with constitutional response to AI, genetic engineering, nanotech, and information technology

keywords: UK Justice and Home Affairs Committee
primary_topics: Address at the 1971-72 Constitutional Convention Surviving Delegates Conference (National Museum of Fine Arts, January 16-18, 2024)

How the 1987 Constitution — drafted during the global 'AI Winter' of 1973-1993 — could not have anticipated the four 'running bulls' of modern technology: AI, genetic engineering, nanotechnology, and information

Seven contemporary technology-society challenges to constitutional governance (in vitro fertilization, nanotechnology disruption, autonomous weapons, cyber risks, social-media-driven youth suicide, illusory truth effect in political ads, 100% mass-media ownership constraint)
The principle that 'humane justice can be dispensed only by judges, not by an automated formula' — defending human-judicial discretion against AI determinism
Endorsement of CJ Gesmundo's Strategic Plan for Judicial Innovations 2022-2027 as the proper SC response to the running bulls
sub_topics: 1987 Constitution timeline: approved by 1986 ConCom Oct 12, 1986; presented to President Cory Aquino Oct 15, 1986; ratified Feb 2, 1987 plebiscite; in force Feb 11, 1987
The AI Winter (1973-1993): US Automatic Language Processing Advisory Committee report (1966) and Lighthill report for UK government (1973) triggered ~20 years of disillusionment in AI
ILOVEYOU worm (May 2000): two young Filipino programmers created malware affecting 10% of internet users, 50M+ computers, $5B+ damages; case dismissed because PH had no anti-malware law at the time
UK House of Lords Justice and Home Affairs Committee Report (March 30, 2022) 'Technology Rules? The Advent of New Technologies in the Justice System' — 'a new Wild West' of AI in policing
Burning question: 'At what point can someone be imprisoned on the basis of a technology that could not be explained?'
Volatile Uncertain Complex Ambiguous (VUCA) era as backdrop
In vitro fertilization and surrogate motherhood — Pope Francis January 8, 2024 declaration as 'despicable' and proposing universal ban
Nanotechnology's dual nature — benefits (computers, power sources, medicine) vs threats (economic disruption, security, privacy, health, environment)
Autonomous weapons: cruise missiles and drones (with human targeting decisions) vs emerging weapons that select and engage targets without human intervention
Cyber risks and AI errors in the hands of terrorists, dictators, warlords — what 'bothers me and keeps me awake at night'
Loneliness epidemic: ~1 in 2 American adults reported loneliness pre-COVID; pandemic exacerbated isolation in age of high screen-time

Social media + youth mental health: suicidality rising; suicide is the second most common cause of death among the young in the US; more die from suicides than from soldiers/terrorists/criminals combined
'Saligang Batas Gawing Saligang Patas' political ad with 1M+ X views; the illusory truth effect (repeated false information becomes believed)
100% mass-media ownership constitutional requirement — debate given CNN/BBC availability and foreign-owned social media platforms
Bletchley Park AI Safety Summit (November 1-2, 2023) — 29 international organizations and governments including Philippines, joint declaration
US CJ John G. Roberts Jr. 2023 Year-End Report on the Federal Judiciary — confidence that judges are not becoming obsolete
CJP's Sept 25, 2023 Inquirer column self-citation on humane justice via judges, not automated formula
Penal Code range-of-penalties + Civil Code 'right and justice' liberality — discretionary tools 'not available to tech bots'

signature_phrases.phrase: ruminations and what keeps me awake at night
humane justice can be dispensed with ONLY BY JUDGES, not always by an automated formula
At what point can someone be imprisoned on the basis of a technology that could not be explained?
there is a Human Mind. There is a Human Heart
scheming lawyers and less than faithful husbands have added three more commandments to the Ten
If caught, thou shall deny, deny, deny, even when caught in flagrante
there is always a right time to do the right time thing, by the right people and for the right reasons
turn tech for good and move from problem to solution

stances.claim: The 1987 Philippine Constitution was drafted during the global 'AI Winter' (1973-1993) — a 20-year trough triggered by the 1966 US ALPAC report and the 1973 Lighthill report for the UK government — and so neither the Constitution itself nor the laws derived from it could have foreseen modern AI, genetic engineering, nanotechnology, or information technology. The proper response is not to scrap the document but to recognize that some provisions (e.g., 100% mass-media ownership) now need reexamination in light of CNN, BBC, and foreign-owned social platforms already operating in the country.
stances.rhetorical_move: historical-context premise establishing the constitutional gap; reform-by-selective-amendment rather than wholesale-replacement

stances.claim: Humane justice can be dispensed ONLY by judges, not by an automated formula. The Penal Code's range of penalties and the Civil Code's 'right and justice' liberality clause confer wide discretion on judges that 'tech bots' cannot replicate. AI may speedily apply rules to facts, but it cannot do justice in the sense required by a just and humane society.
stances.rhetorical_move: discretion-as-irreducibly-human argument with specific statutory anchors
stances.confidence: asserted with self-citation of Sept 25, 2023 Inquirer column

stances.claim: Seven specific challenges deserve constitutional attention: (1) in vitro fertilization and surrogacy (citing Pope Francis's universal-ban call); (2) nanotechnology's dual benefits and risks; (3) autonomous weapons that select and engage without human intervention; (4) cyber risks and AI errors in the hands of terrorists/dictators/warlords; (5) social media-driven youth suicide (suicide is the second most common youth death-cause in the US); (6) the illusory truth effect in political advertising (e.g., 'Saligang Batas Gawing Saligang Patas'); (7) the 100% mass-media ownership requirement.
stances.rhetorical_move: seven-fold enumeration of constitutional-technology pressure points
stances.confidence: asserted as worry-list 'what keeps me awake at night'

stances.claim: The illusory truth effect — repeated false information being believed even when people initially knew it was false — makes Filipino constitutional discourse vulnerable to political-ad manipulation; this risk is amplified by foreign-owned social media platforms; therefore the country must NOT let 'foreign media control the truth.'
stances.rhetorical_move: psychological-research citation deployed against social media information ecosystem
stances.confidence: asserted with named-ad example (Saligang Batas Gawing Saligang Patas)

stances.claim: Even as the running bulls of technology are inevitable and 'here to stay,' the appropriate institutional response of the Philippine judiciary is CJ Gesmundo's Strategic Plan for Judicial Innovations 2022-2027 — to 'turn tech for good and move from problem to solution' — rather than either rejection of technology or surrender to it.
stances.rhetorical_move: endorsement of incumbent CJ's reform program as the proper institutional posture

notable_anecdotes.summary: Opening joke — three new commandments added by 'scheming lawyers and less than faithful husbands': 11th 'Thou shall not get caught'; 12th 'If caught, thou shall deny, deny, deny, even when caught in flagrante'; 13th 'When all else fail, thou shall ask for a fair trial and invoke the constitutional right against self-incrimination and the constitutional right to remain silent.'

ILOVEYOU virus (May 2000): two young Filipino programmers created a worm affecting 10% of all internet users, 50M+ computers, $5B+ damages. The criminal case against them was dismissed not for lack of proof but because the Philippines had no law against malware creation at the time.

Self-positioning: 'I AM NOT A ROBOT. My brain has not absorbed the wealth and breadth of all the information on the internet and the metaverse, but as in the many events in my life, I prayed for discernment.'
Stark statistic: in the US, 'tragically, more people die from suicides than from the combined violence perpetrated by soldiers, terrorists, and criminals.'
Illusory-truth-effect example: a recent political ad 'Saligang Batas Gawing Saligang Patas' garnered ~1 million views on X within days of posting.
Pope Francis on January 8, 2024 (annual ambassadors address): called surrogate motherhood 'despicable' and proposed a universal ban for 'commercialization' of pregnancy.

decision_framework_signals: applies the FACTS-ISSUE-RULING legal-opinion structure to constitutional commentary on technology
uses 'AI Winter' historical-context as the structural explanation for the Constitution's tech-gap
treats human-judicial discretion (statutory range + 'right and justice' liberality) as the irreducible test for humane justice in the AI era
uses seven-fold enumeration of pressure points as worry-list discipline
applies psychological-research findings (illusory truth effect) as analytical lens for constitutional discourse
uses international-best-practices citation (UK House of Lords + Bletchley Park + US Federal Judiciary) as comparative-jurisdictional benchmark
treats reform-by-selective-amendment as the correct posture vs wholesale replacement

one_paragraph_summary: Delivered January 16, 2024 at the National Museum of Fine Arts during the 1971-72 Constitutional Convention Surviving Delegates' Conference and Exhibits on 'Philippine Constitutions and the Social Order,' this speech opens with an admission against interest ('I do not feel qualified... I AM NOT A ROBOT') and a joke about three new commandments added by scheming lawyers and unfaithful husbands ('Thou shall not get caught'; 'deny, deny, deny, even when caught in flagrante'; invoke the right to remain silent). Borrowing the judicial opinion structure (FACTS — ISSUE — RULING), CJP situates the 1987 Constitution — ratified February 2, 1987 and in force February 11, 1987 — within the global 'AI Winter' of 1973-1993, demonstrating with the ILOVEYOU virus case (May 2000, $5B+ damages, two Filipino programmers acquitted because PH had no anti-malware law) that constitutional anticipation of technology was structurally impossible. He then enumerates seven contemporary pressure points: (1) in vitro fertilization and surrogacy (citing Pope Francis's January 8, 2024 ambassadors speech calling surrogacy 'despicable'); (2) nanotechnology's dual nature; (3) autonomous weapons that select targets without human intervention; (4) cyber risks and AI errors in the hands of terrorists, dictators, and warlords; (5) social-media-driven youth suicide (more US young people die by suicide than by soldiers/terrorists/criminals combined); (6) the illusory truth effect in political ads ('Saligang Batas Gawing Saligang Patas' with 1M+ X views); and (7) the 100% Filipino-mass-media ownership constraint in an era of CNN/BBC and foreign social platforms. His ruling: humane justice can be dispensed ONLY by judges, not by automated formula — the Penal Code's range of penalties and the Civil Code's 'right and justice' liberality clause confer discretion irreducible by tech bots. He cites the UK House of Lords March 30, 2022 'Technology Rules?' report ('a new Wild West'), the November 2023 Bletchley Park AI Safety Summit (29 governments including the Philippines), and US CJ Roberts's 2023 Year-End Report confirming judges are not becoming obsolete. He endorses CJ Gesmundo's Strategic Plan for Judicial Innovations 2022-2027 — 'turn tech for good and move from problem to solution' — as the right institutional posture, and closes by invoking the 1987 Constitution's preamble guarantee of human dignity 'especially the poor, the oppressed, the dispossessed, the weak, the disabled and the marginalized' as the irreducible reference point for all technology policy.
